relevantStatutes: California Civil Code § 3300 (damages for breach of contract)
California Civil Code § 3301 (breach occurring in performance of obligation arising from contract)
Civil Code § 1689(b)(4) (rescission for contractor failure to perform)
localOrdinances: San Francisco Building Code (administered by Department of Building Inspection)
San Francisco Consumer & Worker Protection Code (contractor protections)

recentCasePrecedents.name: Lewis Jorge Construction Management, Inc. v. Pomona Unified School Dist.
recentCasePrecedents.relevance: Limits recoverability of lost future profits in construction breach cases; relevant to capping consequential damages beyond direct losses.
recentCasePrecedents.holding: Lost profits on future projects due to impaired bonding capacity not recoverable as they are speculative and not natural result of every construction breach.

recentCasePrecedents.name: Oasis West Realty, LLC v. Goldman
recentCasePrecedents.relevance: Defines material breach allowing termination; applies to contractor abandonment.
recentCasePrecedents.holding: Material breach deprives non-breaching party of substantial benefit of bargain, justifying termination and damages.

statuteOfLimitations.primarySOL: 4 years for written contract breach (Code Civ. Proc. § 337(a))
statuteOfLimitations.analysis: Breach discovered May 20, 2025 (abandonment); SOL runs from discovery or repudiation. Action timely as of Feb 2026 (under 1 year elapsed). Oral claims: 2 years (Code Civ. Proc. § 339).

preSuitRequirements: Send formal demand letter citing contract breach and specifying remedy sought (good faith basis for litigation)
File complaint with Contractors State License Board (CSLB) within 4 years (Bus. & Prof. Code § 7084); mandatory for discipline
No mandatory mediation for Superior Court contract claims under $35k; optional for small claims alternative
14-day notice period as stated in demand aligns with common practice for anticipatory repudiation (Civ. Code § 1689)
availableRemedies: Compensatory damages: unpaid work value, refund of unearned deposit ($42,500 less 30% value), consequential damages ($3,200 temporary costs if foreseeable) (Civ. Code § 3300)
Rescission and restitution of deposit (Civ. Code § 1689(b)(4))
Cost to complete (replacement contractor bid minus remaining contract balance)
Attorney fees if contract provides (Civ. Code § 1717)
CSLB disciplinary action including license suspension and restitution order
localJurisdictionElements: File in San Francisco Superior Court (unlimited jurisdiction >$35k; Code Civ. Proc. § 85)
Unlimited civil case: Complaint (PLD-C-001), Summons (SUM-100), Civil Case Cover Sheet (CM-010)
Filing fee ~$435; serve within 60 days (Code Civ. Proc. § 583.210)
Local rules: Dept. 301-312 for contracts; mandatory settlement conference
CSLB San Francisco office for complaints: 9821 Business Park Dr., Sacramento (handles statewide)
enforcementClimate: CSLB actively enforces contractor abandonments; issues ~1,500 citations/year for home improvement violations, with 70%+ resulting in discipline (2023-2025 data). Superior Court enforces breaches routinely; high success rate for homeowners with documentation (80%+ settlements pre-trial). Recent uptick in enforcement post-2022 housing boom complaints.
commonDefenses: Partial performance justifies deposit retention (quantum meruit for 30% work)
Plaintiff failed to mitigate damages (e.g., not hiring replacement promptly)
Force majeure or owner-caused delays (though facts show unilateral abandonment)
Deposit earned via materials/labor; dispute over work value
riskFlags: Consequential damages ($3,200) may be challenged as unforeseeable (Civ. Code § 3300; Lewis Jorge limits)
Offset for value of partial work (~$25,500) likely required
CSLB complaint may trigger contractor bankruptcy defense
No recent legislative changes; monitor AB 1790 (2025) on contractor bonds
draftingConstraints: Firm tone: Demand full refund + damages within 14 days or CSLB/Superior Court filing
Attach evidence: contract, photos, comms, bank records
Specify causes: breach, rescission, unjust enrichment
Reserve rights; no waiver of claims
Cite Civ. Code §§ 3300, 7159; threaten fees under § 1717
relevantCaselaw: Lewis Jorge Constr. Mgmt., Inc. v. Pomona Unified Sch. Dist., 34 Cal.4th 960 (2004)
Oasis West Realty, LLC v. Goldman, 51 Cal.4th 811 (2011)
proceduralRequirements: Superior Court: e-filing mandatory; proof of service
Discovery: document demands for contractor records
keyLegalPrinciples: Material breach (abandonment) allows termination and damages (Civ. Code § 3300)
Duty to mitigate: plaintiff must show reasonable efforts
Benefit-of-bargain damages: position as if performed
